questions.question: What datasets did 'An Image is Worth 16x16 Words: Transformers for Image Recognition at Scale' use?
questions.gold_answer: ImageNet, ImageNet-21k, CIFAR-100
questions.system_answer: The paper reports pre-training on ImageNet, ImageNet-21k, and JFT-300M. It also evaluates transfer performance on smaller benchmarks including ImageNet, ImageNet-Real, CIFAR-100, and the VTAB suite of 19 tasks.

questions.question: What is the parameter count of the largest variant in 'Training data-efficient image transformers & distillation through attention'?
questions.gold_answer: The largest DeiT variant is DeiT-B★ (DeiT-B with distillation) at 87M parameters.
questions.system_answer: The largest variant listed in the evidence is DeiT-B★, with 87M parameters. The paper’s reference vision transformer is 86M, but the biggest variant shown in the model list is 87M.

questions.question: What architectural innovations does 'Swin Transformer: Hierarchical Vision Transformer using Shifted Windows' introduce?
questions.gold_answer: hierarchical structure, shifted window attention
questions.system_answer: The paper introduces a hierarchical vision Transformer that computes self-attention only within non-overlapping local windows, then alternates with shifted window partitioning to create cross-window connections. It also uses patch merging to build multi-scale feature maps, giving linear computational complexity with respect to image size; the evidence also notes relative position bias as an architectural choice that improves performance.

questions.question: Briefly describe the architecture of 'Emerging Properties in Self-Supervised Vision Transformers'.
questions.gold_answer: DINO is a self-supervised vision transformer training method
questions.system_answer: The paper’s architecture is DINO: a simple self-supervised setup with a student network trained to match a momentum-teacher network across multiple augmented views, using centering and sharpening to prevent collapse. It is applied to both Vision Transformers and convnets, but the paper emphasizes ViTs with small patches as especially effective.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on top-1 accuracy on ImageNet?
questions.gold_answer: No—reported top-1 ImageNet accuracy in this corpus varies widely from 1.3% to 91.0% across 594 results.
questions.system_answer: No. The corpus reports a very wide range of top-1 ImageNet accuracies, from 1.3 to 91.0 (spread 89.7), so the papers do not agree on a single value. There are also conflicting SOTA claims: CoCa (finetuned) is listed at 91.0, while other papers report lower top values such as PaLI-17B at 90.9 and MaxViT-XL at 88.7/88.51.

questions.question: What is the range of top-1 accuracy values reported on CIFAR-100 in this corpus?
questions.gold_answer: The top-1 accuracy on CIFAR-100 in this corpus ranges from 48.5% to 91.88% across 27 results.
questions.system_answer: Across the corpus, reported CIFAR-100 top-1 accuracy values range from 48.5% to 91.88%, a spread of 43.38 percentage points. The highest reported value is 91.88% (AdaptFormer-Swin), and the evidence does not list any explicit SOTA claims for this dataset.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on which model holds SOTA on ADE20K mIoU?
questions.gold_answer: No—this corpus does not agree on a single ADE20K mIoU SOTA model, since 7 papers each claim SOTA on the benchmark.
questions.system_answer: No. The corpus does not fully agree: the highest ADE20K mIoU in the evidence is 62.9 from InternImage-H, but another paper, Vision Transformer Adapter for Dense Predictions, also makes a strong SOTA claim at 61.5 with ViT-Adapter-L. The spread is fairly large overall (26.6 to 62.9), so there are multiple competing high-end claims rather than a single unanimous SOTA model.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on mIoU values on ADE20K?
questions.gold_answer: No—reported ADE20K mIoU values vary widely across 97 results, from 26.6 to 62.9.
questions.system_answer: No. The reported ADE20K mIoU values are not consistent: across 97 results they range from 26.6 to 62.9, with a spread of 36.3 and stddev of 7.29. The corpus also contains multiple conflicting SOTA claims, with different papers reporting top values such as 62.9 (InternImage-H), 61.5 (ViT-Adapter-L), and 59.9 (SwinV2-G).

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on whether position embeddings are necessary for vision transformers?
questions.gold_answer: Papers disagree on the necessity of position embeddings; some ablations show minimal impact while others demonstrate clear performance drops without them.
questions.system_answer: No. The corpus shows disagreement: some papers treat position embeddings as standard or necessary for ViTs, e.g. ViT says it “adds position embeddings,” and CPVT says vision transformers “follow the same fashion to imbue the network with positional information.” But other papers argue they are not strictly necessary or can be removed with little/no loss, e.g. CvT says “Removing position embedding of CvT does not degrade performance,” and the self-supervised ViT study says removing them “only slightly hurts performance.”

questions.question: How did the maximum reported top-1 accuracy on ImageNet change year over year in this corpus?
questions.system_answer: The maximum reported top-1 accuracy on ImageNet rose from 85.2 in 2020 to 90.0 in 2021 and 91.0 in 2022, then declined to 87.8 in 2023 and 83.9 in 2024. Overall, the trend is up from 2020 to a peak in 2022, followed by a downward trend through 2024.
